en.extract:
The wrenthrush (Zeledonia coronata) or zeledonia, is a unique species of nine-primaried oscine, endemic to the Talamancan montane forests. 


Taxonomy and systematics
The wrenthrush is the only member of its genus and family. Despite its name, it is neither a wren (Troglodytidae) nor a thrush (Turdidae), and is not closely related to either of those families. Over time it has been treated as related to thrushes and Old World flycatchers (Muscicapidae) and by the late 20th century was placed as a member of the New World warblers (Parulidae). Phylogenetic studies of nuclear and mitochondrial DNA after that date led to reassessment of several genera and in 2017 the wrenthrush was moved to its own family. The exact placement of that family is still not firmly settled, but most taxonomic systems agree that it is closely related to the spindalises (Spindalidae), Cuban warblers (Teretistridae), and several other small Caribbean families, and more distantly related to New World sparrows (Passerellidae) and New World blackbirds (Icteridae). BirdLife International's Handbook of the Birds of the World places it much closer to the New World sparrows and blackbirds than do the others.
The wrenthrush's genus name commemorates José Castulo Zeledón, a Costa Rican ornithologist. It is monotypic.


Description
The wrenthrush is 10 to 11.5 cm (3.9 to 4.5 in) long and weighs about 21 g (0.74 oz). It is short-tailed and rather plump, with a short thin bill somewhat like that of a Parulidae warbler. The sexes are alike. Adults have a large yellow-orange patch with black borders on their crown. Their face and the sides of their neck are leaden gray. Their upperparts, tail, and flanks are dull olive bronze. Their underparts are leaden gray with an olive tinge to the undertail coverts. Juveniles do not have the orange crown and their upperparts are more brownish than those of adults.


Distribution and habitat
The wrenthrush is found from the Cordillera de Guanacaste in northern Costa Rica discontinuously through that country into western Panama's Chiriquí and Veraguas provinces. It inhabits montane evergreen forest and elfin forest, landscapes characterized by constant wetness and fog. It particularly favors dense vegetation near streams, including stands of Chusquea bamboo. In elevation it ranges from about 1,500 m (4,900 ft) up to treeline; in Costa Rica it mostly occurs around 2,500 m (8,200 ft).


Behavior


Movement
The wrenthrush is essentially sedentary, with minimal elevational movement.


Locomotion
The wrenthrush is a weak flier, seldom taking to the wing and then for only short distances. It has short rounded wings and a small keel to which flight muscles attach, and there was early speculation that it is evolving towards flightlessness.


Feeding
The wrenthrush forages by hopping among branches and along the ground searching for arthropods, especially spiders and Lepidoptera larvae. It mostly forages in dense vegetation, though it occasionally works the edges of it and also hunts as high as 10 m (30 ft) above the ground in vines and epiphytes.


Breeding
The wrenthrush's breeding season appears to span from March to early July. Few nests have been found. They were domes made of moss, twigs, and leaves with a side entrance and a lining of dried leaves, grass, and moss. They were placed in cavities in earthen banks and were partially hidden by overhanging vegetation. The clutch size in three nests was three eggs. The incubation period is not known; the time from hatch to fledging appears to be at least 18 days. Both parents provision the nestlings.


Vocalization
Both sexes of wrenthrush sing, and often as a duet. They are more vocal during the breeding season, and mostly sing at dawn and dusk though they can be heard throughout the day. Their primary song is described as "ssee-del-deet with emphasis on the ending, deet", and it may be sung in a series for several minutes. Their primary call is "a thin pseee".


Status
The IUCN has assessed the wrenthrush as being of Least Concern. It has a small range and its population size is unknown but believed to be stable. No immeditate threats have been identified. "[H]abitat degradation and destruction could become a concern" but "its specialized high-altitude habitat seems not to be presently under threat".


References


Further reading
Hunt, J. H. (1971). "A Field Study of the Wrenthrush, Zeledonia coronata" (PDF). Auk. 88 (1): 1–20. doi:10.2307/4083957. JSTOR 4083957.

The '''wrenthrush''' ('''''Zeledonia coronata''''') or '''zeledonia''', is a unique species of [[nine-primaried oscine]], endemic to the [[Talamancan montane forests]]. 

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The wrenthrush is the only member of its genus and family. Despite its name, it is neither a [[wren]] (Troglodytidae) nor a [[Thrush (bird)|thrush]] (Turdidae), and is not closely related to either of those families. Over time it has been treated as related to thrushes and [[Old World flycatcher]]s (Muscicapidae) and by the late 20th century was placed as a member of the [[New World warbler]]s (Parulidae). [[Phylogenetics|Phylogenetic studies]] of nuclear and mitochondrial DNA after that date led to reassessment of several genera and in 2017 the wrenthrush was moved to its own family. The exact placement of that family is still not firmly settled, but most taxonomic systems agree that it is closely related to the [[spindalis]]es (Spindalidae), [[Cuban warbler]]s (Teretistridae), and several other small Caribbean families, and more distantly related to [[New World sparrow]]s (Passerellidae) and New World blackbirds ([[Icteridae]]).<ref name=NACC>Chesser, R. T., S. M. Billerman, K. J. Burns, C. Cicero, J. L. Dunn, B. E. Hernández-Baños, R. A. Jiménez, A. W. Kratter, N. A. Mason, P. C. Rasmussen, J. V. Remsen, Jr., D. F. Stotz, and K. Winker. 2022. Check-list of North American Birds (online). American Ornithological Society. https://checklist.aou.org/taxa {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200220100331/https://checklist.aou.org/taxa |date=2020-02-20 }}</ref><ref name=IOC12.2>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/enigmas/ |title=Caribbean "tanagers", Wrenthrush, Yellow-breasted Chat |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 12.2 |editor-last1=Gill |editor-first1= F. |editor-last2=Donsker|editor-first2=D.|editor-last3=Rasmussen |editor-first3=P. |date=August 2022 |access-date=9 August 2022 }}</ref><ref name=Clements>Clements, J. F., T. S. Schulenberg, M. J. Iliff, S. M. Billerman, T. A. Fredericks, J. A. Gerbracht, D. Lepage, B. L. Sullivan, and C. L. Wood. 2021. The eBird/Clements checklist of Birds of the World: v2021. Downloaded from https://www.birds.cornell.edu/clementschecklist/download/ Retrieved August 25, 2021</ref> [[BirdLife International]]'s ''[[Handbook of the Birds of the World]]'' places it much closer to the New World sparrows and blackbirds than do the others.<ref name=HBW2021>HBW and BirdLife International (2021) Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International digital checklist of the birds of the world. Version 6. Available at: http://datazone.birdlife.org/userfiles/file/Species/Taxonomy/HBW-BirdLife_Checklist_v6_Dec21.zip retrieved August 7, 2022</ref>

The wrenthrush's genus name commemorates [[José Castulo Zeledón]], a [[Costa Rica]]n [[ornithologist]].<ref name=WREN-BOW>Dupont, G., J. Curson, and A. J. Spencer (2020). Wrenthrush (''Zeledonia coronata''), version 2.0. In Birds of the World (T. S. Schulenberg, S. M. Billerman, and B. K. Keeney, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.wrenth1.02 retrieved October 11, 2022</ref> It is [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC12.2/>

[[File:Wrenthrush - Central Highlands - Costa Rica MG 6965 (26603415282).jpg|thumb|left|Wrenthrush in the Central Highlands of Costa Rica]]
==Description==

The wrenthrush is {{convert|10|to|11.5|cm|in|abbr=on}} long and weighs about {{convert|21|g|oz|abbr=on}}. It is short-tailed and rather plump, with a short thin bill somewhat like that of a Parulidae warbler. The sexes are alike. Adults have a large yellow-orange patch with black borders on their crown. Their face and the sides of their neck are leaden gray. Their upperparts, tail, and flanks are dull olive bronze. Their underparts are leaden gray with an olive tinge to the undertail [[Covert feather|coverts]]. Juveniles do not have the orange crown and their upperparts are more brownish than those of adults.<ref name=WREN-BOW/>

==Distribution and habitat==

The wrenthrush is found from the [[Cordillera de Guanacaste]] in northern Costa Rica discontinuously through that country into western Panama's [[Chiriquí Province|Chiriquí]] and [[Veraguas Province|Veraguas]] provinces. It inhabits montane [[evergreen forest]] and [[elfin forest]], landscapes characterized by constant wetness and fog. It particularly favors dense vegetation near streams, including stands of ''[[Chusquea]]'' bamboo. In elevation it ranges from about {{convert|1500|m|ft|abbr=on}} up to treeline; in Costa Rica it mostly occurs around {{convert|2500|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=WREN-BOW/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The wrenthrush is essentially sedentary, with minimal elevational movement.<ref name=WREN-BOW/>

===Locomotion=== 

The wrenthrush is a weak flier, seldom taking to the wing and then for only short distances. It has short rounded wings and a small [[Keel (bird anatomy)|keel]] to which flight muscles attach, and there was early speculation that it is evolving towards flightlessness.<ref name=WREN-BOW/><ref>Pycraft, W. P. (1905) On the systematic position of ''Zeledonia coronata'', with some observations on the position of the Turdidae. Ibis series 8, volume 5: 1-24</ref>

===Feeding===

The wrenthrush forages by hopping among branches and along the ground searching for [[arthropod]]s, especially spiders and [[Lepidoptera]] larvae. It mostly forages in dense vegetation, though it occasionally works the edges of it and also hunts as high as {{convert|10|m|ft|sigfig=1|abbr=on}} above the ground in vines and [[epiphyte]]s.<ref name=WREN-BOW/>

===Breeding===

The wrenthrush's breeding season appears to span from March to early July. Few nests have been found. They were domes made of moss, twigs, and leaves with a side entrance and a lining of dried leaves, grass, and moss. They were placed in cavities in earthen banks and were partially hidden by overhanging vegetation. The clutch size in three nests was three eggs. The incubation period is not known; the time from hatch to fledging appears to be at least 18 days. Both parents provision the nestlings.<ref name=WREN-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Zeledonia-coronata |species=wrenthrush}}
===Vocalization===

Both sexes of wrenthrush sing, and often as a duet. They are more vocal during the breeding season, and mostly sing at dawn and dusk though they can be heard throughout the day. Their primary song is described as "''ssee-del-deet'' with emphasis on the ending, ''deet''", and it may be sung in a series for several minutes. Their primary call is "a thin ''pseee''".<ref name=WREN-BOW/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the wrenthrush as being of Least Concern. It has a small range and its population size is unknown but believed to be stable. No immeditate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> "[H]abitat degradation and destruction could become a concern" but "its specialized high-altitude habitat seems not to be presently under threat".<ref name=WREN-BOW/>

==References==
{{Reflist}}

zh.extract:
冠鹩森莺（學名：Zeledonia coronata，西班牙語：Reinita de Zeledón，台湾作冠鷯鶇），是雀形目冠鷯鶇科冠鷯鶇屬下的唯一一種鳥類。這種鳥類僅分佈於中美洲的哥斯大黎加及巴拿馬的高山森林區域，主要以昆蟲跟蜘蛛等無脊椎動物為食。由美國鳥類學家羅伯特·里奇韋於1889年首次被描述。
這種鳥類的英文名稱為「Wrenthrush」，雖然有「鷯」（Wren）跟「鶇」（Thrush）的名字，但在經過一連串爭議的分類歷史後，冠鷯鶇既非鷦鷯科，也不是鶇科，而是與森鶯科關係較近。而現在被認為有其獨特性而被獨立為一科。其最顯眼的特徵就是其頭頂上顯眼的銅橘色頭冠，但其生性較為害羞也不善飛，常常隱匿在樹林中而難以發現，通常需仰賴其叫聲分辨牠們。
這個物種因為其很高的分佈地區而暫無明顯的威脅，雖然其分佈區域僅限於一個較小的地區但仍被認為常見，因此在《國際自然保護聯盟瀕危物種紅色名錄》中被列入無危物種。


物種分類與命名
該物種於1889年由美國鳥類學家羅伯特·里奇韋首次進行物種描述，並認為其形態相當獨特。其屬名Zeledonia是為了紀念哥斯大黎加的鳥類學家荷西·卡斯圖洛·澤萊東而建立的。而種小名coronata則是來自拉丁語，意即「有冠冕的」。

因其特殊的外觀形態，早在1889年分類的當下，里奇韋就表示其以至於歸入哪個科下都仍有相當困難，但他認為接近於鸫科下的夜鸫属。到了1905年，英國鳥類學家威廉·普萊恩·皮克拉夫特經由解剖學比較後斷言其「毫無疑問地屬於鶇科成員」，但也描述其「第十枚」初級飛羽相當短小，且尾羽只有十枚，與其他鶇科成員有所不同。里奇韋則因為這些矛盾的描述，在1907年時對皮克拉夫特的分類感到困惑，因而建立了獨立的新分類冠鷯鶇科（Zeledoniidae）。
至此之後，學術界仍不停的爭論著其分類定位：不是歸於鶇科，就是歸於冠鷯鶇科，甚至有研究認為其應屬於燕雀科下的冠鷯鶇族（Zeledoniini），與「森鶯族」（Parulini）並列。自約1960年代開始，冠鷯鶇被歸類至森鶯科內，是一種「異常」的林鶯，並認為與王森莺属最為接近。直到2013年後的研究所提出將冠鷯鶇獨立唯一科的意見被美洲鳥類協會接受，因此自2018年起冠鷯鶇在世界鳥類學家聯合會的名錄中被獨立為一科至今。這個物種也同時為單型屬，沒有亞種變化。


形態描述
冠鷯鶇的體長平均約11.5公分，體重平均約21公克。其鳥喙寬平均3.2公釐、深平均3.6公釐、嘴峰長平均约14.2公釐；翼展公分，翼長平均62.7公分；跗蹠平均長28.3公釐；尾長平均33.4公釐。
這種鳥類是一種顏色灰暗、體型圓胖、尾巴短小的鳥，其特徵是頭頂上的一片金褐頭冠。成鳥的頭頂是鮮艷的古銅橘色，被黑色條紋包圍，鳥喙黑色，瞳孔顏色呈深棕。臉部具有不太明顯的、偏暗的眼紋。上半身為深橄欖綠色；臉部和下半身呈深石板灰色，側面和尾下覆羽帶有橄欖綠色。翅膀和尾巴為深褐色帶深綠色邊緣，該邊緣在覆羽和三級飛羽上佔有最寬的比例。腿褐色。雌雄相似。
幼鳥的亮色頭頂主要被黑灰色羽毛尖端遮蓋，口腔內部為黃色。下半身呈褐色，帶有黑色邊緣，看起來顏色模糊或有鱗片狀的效果。
在解剖學上，其翅膀骨頭發育不良、顱骨頂薄、龙骨突較小。
因其亮色的頭冠，這種鳥類較不會與同個區域內的鳥類搞混，形狀上可能會類似蟻八色鳥，不過顏色明顯不同；若不論頭冠，則在部分情形下跟黑嘴夜鶇最為類似，但黑嘴夜鶇體型較大且尾巴較長。


棲息地與分佈
冠鷯鶇僅分布於哥斯大黎加中央的瓜納卡斯特山脈到巴拿馬西部的濕潤山地森林以及安地斯高山草甸環境。偏好棲息在竹叢茂密的地方，沿著溪流或峽谷在密集的高大樹林中生存，如一些密集的次生林。雖然被描述為「相對常見」的鳥類，但很少被直接看到，通常多由叫聲分辨其存在。多分佈於海拔1500公尺以上的高山地帶，最高可至林木線，密度最高的海拔則是在約2500公尺處。牠們是一種留鳥，並也幾乎不進行垂直方向的海拔遷徙。


習性

冠鷯鶇生性害羞，常神經質地拍動翅膀。似乎是為了適應其躲藏在樹林中的生活方式，其翅膀相當圓潤，與其他林鶯相比時尾巴也相當地小。並導致其飛行能力較差，並且幾乎看不到牠們飛行，多見於牠們在地面上蹦蹦跳跳尋找食物。就算有飛行或驚擾牠們，也頂多作向下的滑翔或只拍動幾下翅膀，並飛不到超過10公尺外的地方，因此被形容其生活方式「類似老鼠」。這種鳥類可能正朝著不會飛行的方向演化。通常會需要耐心及時間等待其身影。


食性
這種鳥類為肉食性，主要食物來源是陸生無脊椎動物的昆蟲。如一些鱗翅目和甲蟲的幼蟲和蛹，以及其他小型昆蟲和蜘蛛等。


繁殖
冠鷯鶇可能全年都會進行交配，但主要會在4月至6月之間。社交上牠們很有可能是一夫一妻制的，雌雄形成的配對似乎會在一整年內都在領地上待在一起。其鳥巢是由苔蘚作成的凹形球體，入口則隱藏在長滿苔蘚和其他植物的垂直土坡的凹處並相當隱密，約需花費8—9天築成。巢建成後約8天後產卵，卵每次2枚，呈白色，有淺褐色斑點。至少在養育17天後幼鳥才離巢。


叫聲
叫聲為高而薄的「tsseee」、「pseee」或「psss」，帶有上升的音調。歌聲則是一個變化多端的短調，由3—5個高而尖銳的哨音組成，如「see-see-suu seeep」或「see-suu-seep」，經常重複。雄鳥和雌鳥都有能力唱歌，並常常一起合唱。


數量與保護情況
其數量被估約有五萬隻成熟個體，並形容其為「相對常見」的鳥類。雖然因其分佈範圍較小，而被列入「受限制分佈物種」之一。但其棲息區被破壞的情形較少，在缺乏任何下降或重大威脅的證據下，被認為其族群趨勢穩定，故在《國際自然保護聯盟瀕危物種紅色名錄》中被列入無危物種。但在其有限的飛行能力下，若未來有入侵種掠食者的問題出現，可能會造成相當大的危害。


註釋


參考資料


外部連結
